title: Koshari

overview.description: Egypt's national dish of rice, lentils, pasta and chickpeas, topped with a rich tomato sauce and crispy onions. A remarkably filling and economical vegetarian meal.

method: Cook the lentils until just tender, then drain.
Cook the basmati rice according to your preferred method.
Cook the pasta until al dente, then drain.
Heat the vegetable oil in a frying pan and slowly fry the sliced onions until deep golden and crisp. Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on kitchen paper.
Reserve a little of the onion oil and gently cook the garlic for 1 minute.
Add the tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes.
Stir in the cumin, coriander and chilli flakes and cook for 30 seconds.
Add the chopped tomatoes and simmer for 15 minutes.
Stir in the vinegar, season with salt and pepper and simmer for another 5 minutes.
To serve, layer the rice, lentils and pasta in bowls, spoon over the chickpeas and tomato sauce, then finish with plenty of crispy onions.

learning.did-you-know: Koshari became popular in Egypt during the 19th century and combines influences from Indian, Italian and Middle Eastern cooking into one iconic national dish.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to coordinate several simple components into one balanced meal and make beautifully crisp fried onions.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: One of the most economical meals in the cookbook while still being hearty, nutritious and completely different from anything else in the planner.
chapman-tips: The crispy onions are essential—don't rush them.
Assemble the dish just before serving to keep each component at its best.
A splash of chilli sauce at the table is traditional.
This is an excellent pantry meal when fresh ingredients are running low.
notes.batch-cooking: Rice, lentils, pasta and sauce all freeze well individually. Assemble after reheating for the best texture.
notes.leftovers: Store the crispy onions separately so they stay crisp.
